title: Week 11 - Sorting Algorithms II and Applications
description: Practice insertion-sort ideas, merging sorted arrays, deduplication, and tied rankings.

questions.title: Insert a New Score Like Organizing Playing Cards

description.background: The teacher has already sorted a group of scores from small to large and stored them in the array scores. A new score newScore arrives and needs to be inserted into the original sorted score sheet while keeping the score sheet sorted from small to large.
description.task: Following the idea of insertion sort, insert newScore into the correct position in the sorted array scores and return the new array after insertion.
description.input: Three parameters: the sorted array scores, the original count n, and the new score newScore.
description.output: Return the new array after insertion.
description.hint: Copy elements smaller than newScore into the result array first, then place newScore, then copy the remaining elements.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(scores, n, newScore)
    local result = {}
    local position = 1
    while position <= n and scores[position] < newScore do
        result[position] = scores[position]
        position = position + 1
    end
    result[position] = newScore
    for i = position, n do
        result[i + 1] = scores[i]
    end
    return result
end

questions.title: Insert a New Student into an Ordered Line

description.background: In PE class, the line is already ordered from short to tall, and all student heights are stored in the array heights. A new student arrives with height newHeight. The teacher needs to insert this student into the correct position so the line remains ordered from short to tall.
description.task: Insert newHeight into the already sorted height array heights, then return the new height array after insertion.
description.input: Three parameters: the height array heights, the original count n, and the new height newHeight.
description.output: Return the height array after insertion.
description.hint: Search from front to back for the first position that is not less than newHeight, put the new height there, and shift the following heights backward.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(heights, n, newHeight)
    local result = {}
    local position = 1
    while position <= n and heights[position] < newHeight do
        result[position] = heights[position]
        position = position + 1
    end
    result[position] = newHeight
    for i = position, n do
        result[i + 1] = heights[i]
    end
    return result
end

questions.title: Find the Middle Score After Sorting

description.background: The teacher has sorted the score sheet from small to large and stored it in the array scores. To understand the middle level of the scores, read the score at the middle position. If the number of students is even, return the left middle score according to the problem rule.
description.task: Find the middle position from n and return the corresponding score in scores.
description.input: Two parameters: the sorted score array scores and the number of students n.
description.output: Return the middle score; for an even number of students, return the left middle score.
description.hint: Use a loop to calculate the middle index without math functions. Each time the count moves forward by two positions, move the middle index forward by one.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(scores, n)
    local mid = 1
    local count = 1
    while count + 2 <= n do
        mid = mid + 1
        count = count + 2
    end
    return scores[mid]
end

questions.title: Merge Two Already Sorted Class Score Sheets

description.background: Two classes have each sorted their score sheets. Arrays a and b are both already sorted from small to large. The teacher needs to merge the two class score sheets into one total score sheet that remains sorted from small to large.
description.task: Merge the two sorted score arrays a and b into one new sorted array, then return the merged result.
description.input: Four parameters: array a, count n, array b, and count m.
description.hint: Use two indexes to point at the current values in the two arrays. Each time, put the smaller value into the result array.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(a, n, b, m)
    local result = {}
    local i = 1
    local j = 1
    local k = 1
    while i <= n and j <= m do
        if a[i] <= b[j] then
            result[k] = a[i]
            i = i + 1
        else
            result[k] = b[j]
            j = j + 1
        end
        k = k + 1
    end
    while i <= n do
        result[k] = a[i]
        i = i + 1
        k = k + 1
    end
    while j <= m do
        result[k] = b[j]
        j = j + 1
        k = k + 1
    end
    return result
end

questions.title: Find the Top Three Scores After Sorting

description.background: After a competition, scores have already been sorted from high to low and stored in the array scores. The scores at the front of the array are the highest scores. Take the top three scores from this sorted score sheet for the award list.
description.task: Read the first 3 scores in scores and save them in original order into a new array top, then return top.
description.input: Two parameters: the descending score array scores and the number of students n.
description.output: Return the array containing the top three scores.
description.hint: The problem guarantees n is at least 3. You can directly loop over scores[1], scores[2], and scores[3].

questions.reference_code:
function solution(scores, n)
    local top = {}
    for i = 1, 3 do
        top[i] = scores[i]
    end
    return top
end

questions.title: Count How Many Different Scores Remain After Sorting

description.background: The teacher has sorted the scores from small to large and stored them in the array scores. Since the array is sorted, equal scores must appear next to each other. Count how many different score values appear in this score sheet.
description.task: Traverse the sorted score array scores, count the number of distinct scores, and return the result.
description.input: Two parameters: the sorted score array scores and the number of students n.
description.output: Return the number of distinct scores.
description.hint: If n is 0, return 0 directly. Otherwise count the first score as one kind, then only increase the count when the current score differs from the previous score.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(scores, n)
    if n == 0 then
        return 0
    end
    local count = 1
    for i = 2, n do
        if scores[i] ~= scores[i - 1] then
            count = count + 1
        end
    end
    return count
end

questions.title: Handle Tied Ranks in a Score Ranking

description.background: Competition scores have been sorted from high to low and stored in the array scores. The leaderboard rule says that students with the same score share the same rank, and later ranks skip according to actual position. For example, if two students tie for rank 1, the next rank is 3.
description.task: Generate the corresponding rank array ranks from the descending score array scores, then return this array.
description.input: Two parameters: the descending score array scores and the number of students n.
description.hint: If the current score equals the previous score, the current rank equals the previous rank; otherwise the current rank equals position i.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(scores, n)
    local ranks = {}
    if n == 0 then
        return ranks
    end
    ranks[1] = 1
    for i = 2, n do
        if scores[i] == scores[i - 1] then
            ranks[i] = ranks[i - 1]
        else
            ranks[i] = i
        end
    end
    return ranks
end

questions.title: Merge Two Sorted Book Number Lists and Keep Them Sorted

description.background: The library has two book number lists, array a and array b, both already sorted from small to large. The administrator needs to merge the two lists into one complete number list while keeping the merged list sorted from small to large.
description.task: Merge the two sorted book number arrays a and b, generate a new sorted number array, and return it.
description.input: Four parameters: array a, count n, array b, and count m.
description.hint: This is the same method as merging two sorted score sheets. Use two indexes to compare the current numbers in the arrays, and put the smaller number into the result array.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(a, n, b, m)
    local result = {}
    local i = 1
    local j = 1
    local k = 1
    while i <= n and j <= m do
        if a[i] <= b[j] then
            result[k] = a[i]
            i = i + 1
        else
            result[k] = b[j]
            j = j + 1
        end
        k = k + 1
    end
    while i <= n do
        result[k] = a[i]
        i = i + 1
        k = k + 1
    end
    while j <= m do
        result[k] = b[j]
        j = j + 1
        k = k + 1
    end
    return result
end

questions.title: Remove Duplicate Numbers After Sorting and Count the Remaining Items

description.background: A group of IDs has already been sorted from small to large and stored in the array ids. Because the array is sorted, duplicate IDs must appear next to each other. Remove duplicates, keep only the first occurrence of each ID, and count how many IDs remain after deduplication.
description.task: Traverse the sorted ID array ids, generate a deduplicated array unique, count the remaining number count, and finally return both unique and count.
description.input: Two parameters: the sorted ID array ids and the count n.
description.output: Return two values: the deduplicated array unique and the remaining item count.
description.hint: Only add the current ID to unique when it is the first element or when it is different from the previous ID.

questions.reference_code:
function solution(ids, n)
    local unique = {}
    local count = 0
    for i = 1, n do
        if i == 1 or ids[i] ~= ids[i - 1] then
            count = count + 1
            unique[count] = ids[i]
        end
    end
    return unique, count
end
